system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: Chord Reference Charts Explanation

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 2 — Chord Reference Charts Explanation

_Pages 5-7._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## open string definition

> The term “open string” refers to the sound the string makes
>
> when no fingers are placed on the neck (finger board).

**Significance (p. 5).** This is the foundational definition that anchors all subsequent fingerboard navigation instruction in the chapter.

## note names between naturals

> In between F and G, G and A,
> A and B, C and D, and D and E, the notes are written as Hate
>
> ic (Gb, Ab. Bb pb, Eb); these same notes could also be called F
> a ot, Ad, , D#. Notice also that every letter name is on
>
> every string at least once.

**Significance (p. 5).** Establishes enharmonic equivalence and the principle that every note name appears on every string.

## memorizing open string names

> 1) You must memorize the open string names (EADGBE)

**Significance (p. 5).** Step one of the method's systematic neck-memorization protocol.

## 1st and 6th string symmetry rule

> 2)Notice that the notes on the 1st and 6th strings are always
> the same letter names on identical frets,

**Significance (p. 5).** Key shortcut halving memory load for outer strings.

## 1st reference points (5th fret)

> on the 5th fret (with the exception of the 3rd string — use the 4th
>
> fret for it) are the same as the open string notes on the next Aigher string (higher in pitch — the
> Ist string is the highest and the 6th string is the lowest.)
>
> Example:
>
> A
> on the Sth fret of the 6th string is A,
> the same sound as A open on the Sth string. eenee
>
> These notes on the 5th fret (3rd string — 4th fret) will be called the Jst reference points.

**Significance (p. 5).** Defines the '1st reference points' concept, the primary landmark for locating notes.

## 2nd reference points (12th fret octave)

> 4) The notes on the 12th fret are the same letter names as the open strings (but an octave higher) and
>
> the notes starting from the |
>
> 3th fret are identical to the notes starting from the Ist fret (but an
>
> octave higher). In other words, the same letter name notes are 12 frets apart on the same string. The
> notes on the 12th fret will be called the 2nd reference points,

**Significance (p. 5).** Defines '2nd reference points' completing the two-landmark neck navigation system.

## finding notes using reference points

> ~Example: Try to find F# on every string. Starting with the 6th string, the reference points are A and
>
> E; ask yourself if F#
>
> is closer to A or E in the alphabet; it is closer to E, so counting up
>
> from E, you find F#.on the 14th fret, remembering that the notes above the 12th fret have
>
> the same letter names
>
> Following this same system
>
> as the notes 12 frets lower, you will find F# also on the 2nd fret.

**Significance (p. 5).** Worked example demonstrating how to apply the two-reference-point system.

## daily practice prescription

> With 15 minutes a
> day of practicing this system, arbitrarily picking any note and trying to find it on all strings, you
> should start to get the picture of the neck in your mind within a short time.

**Significance (p. 5).** Specific practice prescription for internalizing the fingerboard.

## chord chart key choice and transposition

> All chords are given in keys of A and E for convenience, as most guitar players know these keys
> as well or better than others. However, they are all moveable to other keys; for instance, to play a
> C6 you could raise an A6 3 frets or lower an E6 4 frets.

**Significance (p. 6).** Establishes the moveable-chord principle central to the book's reference system.

## silent vs. deadened strings rule

> Only the strings on which notes are listed are to be played. The other strings are either to be
> deadened or are to remain silent.

**Significance (p. 6).** Fundamental chord-voicing rule for reading the book's diagrams.

## finger numbering convention

> The index finger is considered to be the first finger, the middle
> finger the 2nd, and so on. If the thumb were to be used, a T
>
> would indicate it.

**Significance (p. 6).** Defines the fingering notation system used throughout the chord reference section.

## chord tones and major scale relation

> Below eachmajor chord are the “tones” of the chord in relation to the major scale of that chord,
>
> such as root (R), 3rd (3), Sth (5). If you do not understand this, go right to section 4 of this book.

**Significance (p. 6).** Links chord diagrams to chord-formula theory.

## 1st finger split-fret technique

> Also notice that in some of the charts you will have the Ist finger indicated on 2 different
> frets like so:
> Ina case like this the 4 ‘
> note on the 2nd string is played with
>
> the tip of the 1st finger a f rc
>
> while the note on the Ist string is played with the side of
> the 2nd or 3rd joint of the same finger. This technique takes a little practice but is not too difficult
> once you get into it.

**Significance (p. 6).** Specific left-hand technique using different joints of the same finger.

## chord symbol conventions (slash, plus, augmented)

> 1) A
> slash through a number means major type. Example:.2= major 9th; *= major 7th, etc. 2) The symbol
> that looks like a plus sign (+) always means that the 5th of a chord is sharped (in this book), unless it
> is written like so (+11) . This means the 11th is sharped; the word
> augmented is sometimes used instead of sharped.

**Significance (p. 7).** Defines the book's chord symbol notation rules.

## suspended chord definition

> Also the word suspended in this book will mean that the 3rd in a chord has been replaced with a
> 4th. In other books, it may be used instead of the word add. For instance, C sus. 9 could be C add 9.
> In any case, it is abbreviated sus.

**Significance (p. 7).** Book-specific definition of 'suspended,' a critical disambiguation.

## chord slang terms defined

> 1) The DOMINANT 7th chord is usually just called the “7th” chord.
> 2) The DIMINISHED 7th chord is usually just called the “diminished” chord.
> 3) The MAJOR 6th and MAJOR 6/9 chords are usually just called the “6” and “6/9” chords.
> 4) The MAJOR add 9th chord is usually just called the “add 9” chord.

**Significance (p. 7).** Catalog of conventional abbreviations used throughout the book.

## chord TYPE category definition

> The word “TYPE” of chord will refer to a whole category, or a good portion of it.
>
> Example: A C major Tee means C6, C7, C9, C6/9, etc. A Cm TYPE means Cm6, Cm7, Cm7,
>
> Cm7/11, Cm7. 5, etc. However, a Cm7 TYPE would refer only to chords that are derived
> from the m7, Some Cm7 types are Cm7, Cm7/11, Cm9, Cm11, Cm755, Cm9b5 and Cm13.

**Significance (p. 7).** Defines the 'TYPE' terminology used throughout the method.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
